title: Magnetic-field-tunable cyclotron hyperbolic polaritons
authors: Zijian Zhou, Ran Jing, Heng Wang, Lukas Wehmeier, Mengkun Liu, Bing Cheng
abstract: Hyperbolic polaritons are conventionally associated with structural anisotropy or phononic Reststrahlen bands. Here, we predict a new class of hyperbolic polaritons arising from magnetic-field-induced cyclotron motion of charge carriers. When a perpendicular magnetic field is applied to high-mobility semimetals, the cyclotron response drives the in-plane dielectric function from metallic- to insulating-like below the cyclotron resonance frequency, while the out-of-plane response remains metallic. This anisotropy creates a hyperbolic dielectric environment that supports field-tunable hyperbolic polaritons. We develop a comprehensive theoretical framework incorporating coupling to other collective excitations and show that these modes can be directly visualized in real space via terahertz near-field nanoscopy. Our work identifies cyclotron motion as a new route to hyperbolic polaritons and establishes a versatile platform for magnetically programmable nanophotonics.
summary: The paper introduces a new class of hyperbolic polaritons that are tunable via an external magnetic field rather than fixed crystal structures. By inducing cyclotron motion in high-mobility semimetals, the researchers show that the dielectric anisotropy can be controlled to create programmable nanophotonic devices. This discovery opens new avenues for reconfigurable terahertz-frequency light-matter interfaces.
main_problem: Traditional hyperbolic polaritons rely on fixed structural or phononic anisotropy, making them difficult to tune. The authors aim to demonstrate a new class of hyperbolic polaritons that can be dynamically tuned via an external magnetic field.
main_result: The study predicts and models magnetic-field-induced hyperbolic polaritons in high-mobility semimetals, where cyclotron motion creates a tunable anisotropic dielectric environment. This provides a platform for magnetically programmable nanophotonics and the study of light-matter coupling.
method: The authors use a Drude model for low-energy optical conductivity and a dielectric tensor approach to solve Maxwell's equations. They employ the transfer matrix method for reflection coefficients and simulate THz near-field nanoscopy (sSNOM) to visualize wave packets.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of high-mobility semimetal thin films, such as Cd3As2, subjected to a perpendicular magnetic field. The model utilizes a 3x3 anisotropic dielectric tensor that includes a Hall component induced by cyclotron resonance.
key_observables: Cyclotron resonance frequency, circularly polarized conductivity, dielectric functions (exx, exz), reflection coefficients (rpp, rps), polariton dispersion, and spacetime maps of wave packets.
important_parameters: Magnetic field strength (up to 7 T), frequency range (up to 7.5 THz), film thickness (100 nm to 3500 nm), and cyclotron resonance frequency (nu_cr).
main_assumptions: Assumes in-plane isotropy (exx = exyy) and uses the Drude model for the low-energy optical conductivity regime.
figures_summary: Figure 3 shows the simulated dielectric function including phonon coupling and the resulting polariton dispersion. Figure 4 presents the incident THz spectrum and simulated spacetime maps of hyperbolic polariton wave packets at different cyclotron frequencies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of magnetic-field-tunable polaritons, establishes a theoretical framework using a dielectric tensor, presents numerical simulations of dispersion and spacetime dynamics, and discusses experimental detection via THz-sSNOM and potential applications in nanophotonics.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and cavity QED as it proposes a new, dynamically programmable platform for controlling light-matter interactions and exploring strong-coupling regimes in the terahertz range.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Magnetic-field-driven cyclotron hyperbolic polaritons. (a) Schematic of magnetic-field–driven terahertz hyperbolic polaritons in a high-mobility semimetal. A magnetic field applied along the z-axis confines electron cyclotron motion to the xy-plane. A deposited gold film launches the polariton mode, which can be detected by terahertz scattering-type near-field microscopy through a line scan along the black line. (b) Real part of the circularly polarized terahertz conductivity σcirc of Cd3As2 thin films under magnetic field. (c,d) Simulated Re(ϵxx) and Re(ϵzz) at zero and finite magnetic fields up to 7.5 THz. Insets show the corresponding isofrequency contours at 2.5 THz. (e–h)...

FIG. 3. Coupling between field-driven cyclotron hyperbolic polaritons and lattice vibrations. (a) Numerical simulations of the dielectric function including both an IR-active phonon at 2.5 THz and a cyclotron resonance mode at 6 THz. (b) The numerical simulations of polariton dispersion for a 100- nm-thick semimetal film including the phonon effect.

FIG. 2. Influence of Hall response on the polariton dispersion. (a,b) Numerical simulations of the reflection coefficient rpp for a 3500-nm-thick semimetal film computed at vcr = 6 THz, with and without the Hall component ϵxy. (c) Representative line cut at v = 3 THz, taken along the dashed black lines in panels (a) and (b). (d) Thickness dependence of the shift ratio induced by the Hall response ϵxy.

FIG. 4. Spacetime map of the field-driven cyclotron hyperbolic polaritons. (a) The THz pulse’s spectrum, and momentum- coupling weight function which describes the near-field probe’s coupling strength to momentum used in the simulation of spacetime map. (b-d) The simulated spacetime maps of the hyperbolic polariton wave packet for CR frequency vcr = 3, 4, 5, and 6 THz. The inset highlights the fixed-phase wavefronts. The black line with arrows indicates the directions of the group velocity vg and phase velocity vp, respectively.
